steps.title: Find the key signal
steps.content: The sentence presents two connected ideas: an observation (the supernova dimmed over a few weeks) and a clarifying explanation (supernovas are short-lived despite being powerful). The word “although” signals that the second part explains or contextualizes the first. We need punctuation that properly marks the transition between these two related thoughts.

steps.title: Match the meaning
steps.content:
When “however” is used as a parenthetical transition word to bridge an observation and its explanatory clause, the pattern is IC, transition; explanatory clause — a comma before the transition word and a semicolon after it, which creates the necessary separation before the dependent clause. Option B uses weeks, however; which correctly applies this punctuation structure. Options A and C place “However” and “although” in succession, creating an awkward double-contrast that muddies the logical flow; option D treats “however” as a mid-sentence interrupter without the semicolon needed to separate it from the following dependent clause.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
